PRO HAC VICE RENEWAL FEE CALENDAR FOR ATTORNEY WILLIAM P. BRESNAHAN
Off Calendar Sep-25-2019 Inactivate Attorney

PRO HAC VICE RENEWAL FEE STATUS FOR ATTORNEY WILLIAM P. BRESNAHAN
Off Calendar Aug-14-2019 Continued To Sep-25-19 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 206

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees
Off Calendar Feb-04-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Danieal H. Dressman, Esq., Gordon & rees, Telephone 415-875-4303, for Plaintiff;
Tara M. Steeley, Esq., Deputy City Attorney CCSF, Telephone 415-554-4655 appearing for Defendant CCSF;

Ruling - ARGUED. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PETITIONER KHP III SUTTER LLC A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees

Judge Schulman discloses that he is professionally acquainted with Don Willenburg, one of Petitioner's counsel. This will not affect his ability to fairly and impartially decide this matter.

Petitioner KHP III SF Sutter LLC's motion for an award of attorneys' fees under Code of Civil Procedure section 1021.5 is denied. While a taxpayer's due process right to appeal from a real property tax assessment undoubtedly is an important right affecting the public interest, the necessity and financial burden of private enforcement are not such as to make the requested award appropriate. "It is within the trial court's discretion to deny attorneys' fees pursuant to section 1021.5 on the ground that the plaintiff's personal stake in the outcome was not disproportionate to the burden of private enforcement, even where the litigation enforced an important right and conferred a significant benefit upon the public." (Satrap v. Pacific Gas & Electric Co. (1996) 42 Cal.App.4th 72, 78 [collecting authorities].) KHP bears the burden of establishing that its litigation costs transcend its personal interest. (Beach Colony II v. California Coastal Comm'n (1985) 166 Cal.App.3d 106, 112-114.) It has not carried that burden. Petitioner had a very substantial financial interest in filing an administrative appeal from a $44.6 million supplemental assessment. Petitioner's position is that the property should have been assessed at $30.6 million, which if accepted would result in very substantially lower property taxes. (Dressman Dec., Ex. A, Admin. Record, pp. 3, 71.) Thus, KHP's personal financial stake in the outcome was not so disproportionate to the cost of the litigation (for which it seeks a total of $55,961.50 in attorneys' fees) that the writ of mandate proceeding would not have been brought without the additional incentive of an award of attorneys' fees.

Petitioner's motion for an award of attorneys' fees in the amount of $7,500 under Government Code section 800 is granted. The Board's dismissal of Petitioner's appeal as untimely was arbitrary and capricious. An agency's decision may be found to be arbitrary and capricious when no substantial evidence supports the agency's actions or where there has been " 'a stubborn insistence on following an unauthorized course of action.' " (Campbell v. Residential Rent Stabilization and Arbitration Board (1983) 142 Cal.App.3d 123, 128-129.) Both conditions apply here. In granting the writ, the Court explicitly found that "[t]he Board's determination that KHP's reliance on the language in the Instructions was unreasonable constitutes prejudicial abuse of discretion because it is not supported by substantial evidence in the administrative record." Further, the Court found that the Board was equitably estopped from rejecting KHP's appeal of the supplemental assessment because it affirmatively advised KHP that it could file an appeal from the supplemental assessment within 60 days of the tax bill, and then refused to consider that appeal. Counsel for the petitioner is to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling. Order was submitted in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600 email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

Notice Of Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus
Off Calendar Oct-15-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Dan H. Dressman, Esq. of Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani, 415.875.4303, appearing for the Petitioner;
No appearance by any other party or cousnel.

PETITIONER KHP III SUTTER LLC A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY NOTICE OF PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDAMUS.

There being no other appearance, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows: Petitioner KHP III SF Sutter LLC's petition for writ of administrative mandamus is granted. "[W]here a government agent's actions have induced noncompliance with procedural requirements, the government could be estopped from asserting those procedural bars against a citizen's recovery." (J.H. McKnight Ranch, Inc. v. Franchise Tax Bd. (2003) 110 Cal.App.4th 978, 979.) Respondent Assessment Appeals Board affirmatively advised KHP by the language in its "Information and Instructions for Assessment Appeal Application" that KHP could file an appeal to the supplemental assessment within 60 days of the date of the tax bill. The Board is therefore equitably estopped from rejecting KHP's appeal of the supplemental assessment. Contrary to the Board's assertions, estoppel applies because the equitable remedy is necessary to avoid a "grave injustice" to KHP in losing its chance to contest the accuracy of the $44,600,000 tax bill assessed against it. (Steinhart v. County of Los Angeles (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1298, 1315.) "A taxpayer has a right to a hearing on his property tax assessment, and if an application for a hearing is denied for insufficient legal reason there is a denial of due process." (Midstate Theatres, Inc. v. Board of Supervisors (1975) 46 Cal.App.3d 204, 208.) The Board's determination that KHP's reliance on the language in the Instructions was unreasonable constitutes prejudicial abuse of discretion because it is not supported by substantial evidence in the administrative record. (CCP 1094.5(b), (c).) Nothing in the notice of supplemental assessment mailed to KHP eliminates or remediates the harmful consequences of the now repudiated language contained in the Instructions that was relied on by KHP. That the Board subsequently obtained approval from the State Board of Equalization to correct the language relied on by KHP is tantamount to a confession of error by the Board and compels the conclusion that KHP not be denied the critical right to appeal the supplemental assessment because it relied on erroneous information disseminated by the Board. KHP is entitled to a writ of mandate requiring the Board to accept KHP's administrative appeal as timely and to determine the merits of that appeal. The order and judgment are signed in open court. Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR 8600, 415.551.5868, Email: torreanom@aol.com. =(302/HEK)
